title: Borrowing & References 🤝
description: Lending your data out without giving up control.

slides.title: The Function Trap 🪤
slides.content:
Because of Move semantics, passing a variable into a function instantly steals its ownership!

```rust
let s = String::from("hello");
calculate_length(s); // Ownership passed into the function
// ERROR! `s` is now DEAD. You cannot print it here.
```
This gets incredibly annoying. How do we let a function view data without giving away ownership?

slides.title: Borrowing! 🤝
slides.content:
We use **References** and **Borrowing**.

A reference allows you to refer to a value without taking ownership of it. You denote a reference with the ampersand `&` symbol.

```rust
let s1 = String::from("hello");
let len = calculate_length(&s1); // We pass a REFERENCE
println!("The string '{}' has length {}", s1, len);
```

quizData.question: What symbol is used to create a reference that 'borrows' data instead of taking ownership?
quizData.explanation: The strict ampersand `&` creates an immutable reference, meaning it's pointing to the data but does not own it.

slides.title: Immutable by Default 🔒
slides.content:
Just like standard variables, References are entirely *immutable* by default!

```rust
fn calculate_length(s: &String) {
    s.push_str(", world"); // ERROR! You cannot modify a borrowed read-only value
}
```
If you lend someone your bicycle, you don't expect them to paint it red.

slides.title: Mutable References 🎨

quizData.question: The strict Rule of Borrowing states:

options.text: You can have exactly one mutable reference, OR infinite immutable references at the same time. Never both.
You can have as many mutable references simultaneously as you want.
References must be larger than 50 bytes.

quizData.explanation: This guarantees that if data is being heavily read simultaneously by hundreds of threads, absolutely NO thread is allowed to mutate it under the hood. Perfect Concurrency!
